preFix.title: Using winding sticks to check timber for twist
preFix.subtitle: Detect warp and wind in a board using two parallel reference sticks
preFix.excerpt: How to use a pair of winding sticks to reveal whether a timber board is in wind: twisted along its length: including making a pair of sticks, reading the result, and deciding whether the board can be corrected by hand planing or is too far out to use.
preFix.sourceNotes: The Complete Manual of Woodworking, Albert Jackson and David Day. Hand Tool School published guides. Traditional cabinet-making reference.

content.text: reveal twist in a board that the naked eye cannot detect, especially on longer pieces where the warp is spread over a metre or more. A board
cannot be used as a face side for joinery: it will transfer its twist into the finished piece. Winding sticks are used at the start of any hand-planing operation that requires a true face.
Step 1, make or choose the sticks

Winding sticks must be straight and parallel: they do not need to be elaborate. Two lengths of planed softwood batten (approximately 350 × 50 × 6 mm), straightened with the smoothing plane and checked by eye for straightness, work as well as turned hardwood. A contrasting-colour inlay strip on the top edge of one stick (a piece of white tape or a routed groove filled with light wood) makes the far stick more visible when sighting along the board.
Step 2, place the sticks and sight along the board

Place one stick across the near end of the board and one stick across the far end, each centred across the width of the board. Crouch down until your eye is at the level of the near stick's top edge. Look along the board at the top edge of the far stick. If the board is flat, both top edges will appear as a single line: or parallel. If the board is
, one end of the far stick will appear raised above the near stick top edge at the same side: that raised corner is the

Mark both high corners with chalk or pencil: they are always at opposite diagonal corners of the board (top-left and bottom-right, or top-right and bottom-left). These are the corners that must be planed down. The two low corners must not be touched.
Step 4, correct the twist by planing

Work diagonally across the board with the smoothing plane, concentrating the cuts at the two high corners. Take light, consistent shavings and stop to re-check with the winding sticks after every 5 to 10 passes. The sticks will show the wind reducing as material comes off the high corners. Stop when the far stick's top edge appears in line with the near stick.

Winding sticks can also be used to check assembled frames and carcase furniture for twist before final glue-up. Place the sticks across the front edge at the top and bottom of the piece; if the sticks sight parallel, the assembly is not in wind. For machine-planed timber, wind is rare but still present in timber from small sawmills or in boards that have moved after milling: checking before beginning any joint-cutting takes less than a minute.

attrs.fix: Plane both sticks flat and test them against each other: place them together top-to-top and hold up to the light — no light should show between the edges. True up any gaps with the plane.
attrs.cause: One winding stick is not straight along its top edge; the sticks are themselves in wind.
attrs.symptom: Far stick appears to slope even though the board surface looks flat

attrs.fix: Move the winding sticks to different positions along the board to map where the high and low points are. A cupped board is corrected by planing across the grain at the high points rather than diagonally.
attrs.cause: A cup or localised bow rather than a full-length twist.
attrs.symptom: Board appears in wind at the far end but not the near end — wind is localised

attrs.fix: Re-check with the winding sticks and a straightedge together. Use the straightedge along and across the board to find any remaining high points; plane until both instruments confirm the surface is true.
attrs.cause: High corners reduced but low corners not at the same level as the corrected area; or too much material removed in one diagonal direction.
attrs.symptom: After correcting the twist, the face side is no longer flat when checked with a straightedge

initialFindings.message: TECHNIQUE tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
